column2: A user reports "Cannot read from the source disk" while copying critical files from a laptop drive. What should you prioritize before attempting repair steps?
column3: Protect the user's data before attempting risky repair.
column4: Preserve the data by getting a backup or verified copy if the drive is still readable.
column5:
Why

Read/write failures may get worse during troubleshooting. A repair command or heavy diagnostic can stress a failing drive, so the support decision is to protect recoverable data before taking riskier action.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic HighYield Storage Backup Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop is extremely slow and its storage activity light stays on almost constantly. What theory should you test before replacing memory or the CPU?
column3: Look for evidence that the storage device is struggling before blaming other components.
column4: Test whether the storage device is retrying reads/writes or has unusually slow disk I/O.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Constant storage activity can point to repeated access attempts or a storage bottleneck. Measure the storage path first so the repair plan is based on evidence instead of guessing at unrelated components.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.51; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic Performance Storage Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A laptop hard drive makes a loud grinding noise and the user says files open slowly. What should you avoid doing first?
column3: Noisy storage plus slow access raises the data-risk level.
column4: Avoid running write-heavy repair attempts before protecting the data or escalating for recovery.
column5:
Reasoning

Grinding or clicking from a mechanical drive is a serious failure clue. The interview-friendly answer is to stop treating it like a routine software issue, preserve data if possible, and avoid actions that could make recovery harder.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic HighYield HDD Backup Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A workstation displays a "Boot Device Not Found" style message. What should a technician verify before assuming Windows is corrupted?
column3: First decide whether the system can see the storage device at all.
column4: Verify whether the drive is detected and check physical indicators, cables, power, firmware settings, and the exact boot error.
column5:
Narrowing

This symptom can happen before the operating system is involved. The first split is drive detection versus bootability: if the system cannot see the device, Windows repair is premature.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic HighYield Boot Storage Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Firmware detects the internal drive, but startup stops with an "Operating system not found" style message. What does that evidence suggest?
column4: The storage device may be present, but the operating system or boot data is not usable.
column5:
Why

Drive detection changes the theory. A missing OS message is not the same as a missing drive, so the next work should focus on boot configuration, boot files, or OS state after data risk is considered.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic Boot OS Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: After a desktop was moved to a new desk, it no longer detects its internal SATA drive. What is the best first physical check?
column3: Recent movement makes a simple physical cause more likely.
column4: Check the drive data and power cable connections for looseness or damage.
column5:
Help Desk Logic

The timing points to a physical connection problem. Cable checks are fast, reversible, and less disruptive than reinstalling the OS or replacing the drive.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic HighYield Cables Boot Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A PC keeps trying to boot from a USB recovery drive instead of the internal disk. What is the best first fix?
column3: Rule out the simplest boot-source mistake before changing hardware.
column4: Remove the unintended bootable USB media and confirm the BIOS or UEFI boot sequence if needed.
column5:
Exam Trap

Wrong boot media can look like a storage failure. Start with the simple cause, then verify boot order if the symptom repeats.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic HighYield Boot Firmware Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A newly installed internal drive does not appear during OS setup. Cables are seated. What should you check next?
column3: For a new install, verify configuration and detection before declaring the drive bad.
column4: Check the hardware configuration, including whether the storage interface is enabled and whether another compatible interface or port detects the drive.
column5:
Why

New-installation failures can come from configuration, interface, or port problems. This tests the detection path before deciding the drive itself is bad.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic Installation Firmware Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: An internal drive is not detected after cable and firmware checks. What known-good test can help separate a bad drive from a bad system connection?
column3: A known-good path helps show whether the problem follows the drive.
column4: Try the drive in another compatible computer or known-good storage connection.
column5:
What It Rules Out

Testing through a different path helps isolate whether the failure follows the drive or stays with the original computer, cable, port, or power path.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic KnownGood Storage Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: An SSD can still read some files but fails whenever users save changes. What should the technician do before trying aggressive repairs?
column3: Do not rely on the absence of mechanical noise when judging SSD risk.
column4: Back up readable data and treat the drive as potentially failing.
column5:
Why

SSD failure may not include noise. Read-only behavior or sudden write failure is enough reason to protect data and plan replacement or escalation before trying steps that could reduce access further.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic HighYield SSD Backup Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A server storage array is beeping and reports a RAID warning. What should you do before pulling any drives?
column3: Use array evidence before taking physical action.
column4: Check the RAID management console to identify the affected array, volume, and drive status.
column5:
Safety

RAID arrays vary. Pulling the wrong drive can turn a degraded array into an outage or data-loss event. Console evidence comes before physical action.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic HighYield RAID Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: You must escalate a RAID failure to the infrastructure team. What information should your ticket include?
column3: Escalation should capture the array state and data-risk context.
column4: Include the exact alert, array or volume affected, console status, failed disk identifier if known, recent power or communication issues, backup status, and steps already taken.
column5:
Professional Practice

Escalation should let the next technician continue without repeating discovery. For RAID, the console details and backup status are especially important because the wrong recovery action can affect multiple drives or volumes.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic RAID Escalation Documentation Source::Messer-v170

column2: Monitoring software shows worsening S.M.A.R.T. warning values for a storage drive. What is the best next step?
column3: Treat health warnings as a chance to act before users lose access.
column4: Verify a good backup and plan to replace the drive before it fails completely.
column5:
Reasoning

S.M.A.R.T. is useful because it can show warning signs before total failure. Treat an increasing warning trend as evidence to act early, not as trivia to memorize by attribute number.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.51; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic HighYield SMART Backup Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A NAS sends repeated storage health notifications over email. Why should those alerts be handled before users lose access to files?
column3: Repeated warnings are an opportunity for planned action instead of emergency recovery.
column4: They may indicate a drive problem that can be backed up and replaced before it becomes a data-loss outage.
column5:
Interview Angle

Good support responds to warning signs while there is still time to protect data. Waiting until the share is unavailable turns planned maintenance into an incident.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.51; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic SMART Monitoring Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user says a database app is slow, and you suspect storage latency. What kind of evidence helps test the storage-performance theory?
column3: Measure the suspected bottleneck before trusting a vague performance complaint.
column4: Measure storage access performance, such as I/O operations per second, instead of relying only on the user's description.
column5:
Why

Read/write delays can occur in many parts of the path. A storage metric helps separate a drive or storage bottleneck from an application, CPU, or memory theory.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.51; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic IOPS Performance Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Windows boots normally, but a secondary internal drive no longer appears in the OS. What should you check first?
column3: Because the OS still boots, focus on whether the missing drive is detected.
column4: Check whether firmware detects the drive, then inspect the drive's data and power connections.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

Because the OS boots, the missing item is the secondary storage device. Firmware detection and internal cabling narrow the issue before file-system or OS repair is attempted.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.51; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic HighYield MissingDrive Cables Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user's external drive disappeared from the OS after they moved their laptop. What should you check before assuming the drive failed?
column3: Rule out simple external power and connection issues first.
column4: Check external drive power, the cable connection, and the port or adapter path.
column5:
Why

External storage depends on power and a removable connection. Movement makes a loose cable or missing power path more likely than sudden media failure.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.51; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic ExternalDrive Cables Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user logs in and their mapped network drive is missing, but local disks are present. What should you verify first?
column3: Decide whether this is local storage or a mapped network resource.
column4: Verify network availability and whether the share is set to reconnect at sign-in or through a login script.
column5:
Distinction

A missing network share is not the same as a failed local disk. The correct starting point is the mapping and sign-in behavior, not opening the computer to replace storage.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.51; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic NetworkShares Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: You cannot resolve a storage issue and must escalate it. What storage-specific details should you document?
column3: Include what proves the issue is storage-related and how much data is at risk.
column4: Document the exact error, drive detection status, noises or lights, cable and power checks, S.M.A.R.T. or diagnostic results, backup status, and data-risk concerns.
column5:
Interview Angle

This is the difference between "the drive is broken" and a useful escalation. Storage tickets should make the risk to data clear and show what evidence was already gathered.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.51; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.2
column10: A+::220-1201::5.2 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingStorageDevices Basic HighYield Escalation Documentation Interview Source::Messer-v170
